svg.text: SHONA
HISTORY & SOUTHERN AFRICA
SHONA ORIGINS
Shona peoples: descended from Iron Age Bantu migrations (~300 CE)
~1100-1450 CE: Great Zimbabwe — Shona-built stone city complex
Great Zimbabwe: 11m walls without mortar; largest in sub-Saharan Africa
~1430-1760: Mutapa Kingdom (Monomotapa) — gold trade with coast
~1690-1900: Rozvi Empire — successor Shona state
COLONIAL PERIOD
1890: British South Africa Company (Rhodes) colonizes Zimbabwe
1931: Clement Doke standardizes Shona (Union Shona) from dialects
1965: Rhodesia UDI; Shona speakers join liberation movement
1980: Zimbabwe independence; Shona becomes official language
~15M speakers; 70-75% of Zimbabwe's population speaks Shona
NYANJA HISTORY · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
MARAVI KINGDOM & LAKE NYASA
Lake Nyasa = Lake Malawi: "nyasa" = lake in Nyanja
~1480-1700: Maravi Kingdom — Chewa/Nyanja people confederation
Maravi traded ivory and slaves to Swahili coast merchants
David Livingstone: called it "Lake Nyasa" (1859) from local name
COLONIAL STANDARDIZATION
1875: Scottish missionaries create first Nyanja written texts
Nyanja Bible translation 1922 — major literary milestone
Malawi independence 1964: Chichewa (Nyanja) becomes national language
Zambia: Nyanja is one of 7 official regional languages
NYANJA TODAY
~15M speakers across Malawi, Zambia, Mozambique, Zimbabwe
Malawi: Chichewa is ONE of two official languages (with English)
Lusaka (Zambia capital): Nyanja is primary street language
SHARED SOUTHERN BANTU HERITAGE
Both: descended from Bantu migrations into southern Africa
Both: Great Zimbabwe was within reach of both peoples
Both: sophisticated pre-colonial kingdoms (Mutapa, Maravi)
Shared word: "nyika" (Shona) / "dziko" (Nyanja) both mean "land"
Zimbabwe-Malawi shared border: Shona and Nyanja speakers meet
PLATE 36d ALT · SHONA / NYANJA · HISTORY
